Notice Of Motion And Motion By Defendants For Leave To Aemand An Independent Psychiatric Medical Examination Of Plaintiff Erica Yingling; Memorandum Of Points And Authorities; And Declaration Of Alexander Cheung, In Support Thereof
Off Calendar Feb-03-2025 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES: No appearances. The Court adopts the tentative ruling as follows:

DEFENDANTs AVALONBAY COMMUNITIES, INC., AVALON AT MISSION BAY II, and MVP I, LLC.'s Motion By Defendants For Leave To Amend An Independent Psychiatric Medical Examination Of Plaintiff Erica Yingling.

Defendants AVALONBAY COMMUNITIES, INC. and AVALON AT MISSION BAY II, MVP I, LLC's Motion By Defendants For Leave To Amend An Independent Psychiatric Medical Examination Of Plaintiff Erica Yingling is DENIED.

CCP 2032.020 provides that any party may obtain discovery by means of a physical or mental examination of a party, in any action in which the mental or physical condition of that party is in controversy in the action. "As used in Code of Civil Procedure section [2032.020], the word 'condition' means 'state of being.' (Webster's Collegiate Dict. (10th Ed.1993) p. 240.) Logically, the statute's reference to a party's mental 'state of being' could only refer to the party's current mental state rather than mental conditions which had been experienced in the past but were no longer in existence." (Doyle v. Superior Court (1996) 50 Cal.App.4th 1878, 1886.) "In the absence of an allegation that [Plaintiff] had any current mental injury as a result of [Defendant's] conduct, her present mental condition was not directly relevant to her allegation that she had suffered emotional distress in the past." (Id.) Here, Plaintiff has disclaimed any intent to seek damages for emotional distress other than past emotional distress. (Opp., 9:4-6; Ex. A, 190-192) Thus, an order compelling an examination into her current mental state is not appropriate.

Defendants argue that the degree of Plaintiff's emotional distress has put her mental condition in controversy. (Reply 2:10-12; 3:23-25.) Not so. When the defendant in Doyle sought to test the extent of the plaintiff's alleged distress, the court held the only statutorily authorized justification for ordering a mental examination is that the "mental condition" of the examinee is "in controversy." (Doyle, 50 Cal.App.4th at 1886.) And, here, again, Plaintiff states her current mental state is not in controversy.

Counsel for the prevailing party is required to prepare a proposed order which repeats verbatim the court's ruling and must drop it in Dept. 302's inbox.

Judge: Joseph M. Quinn, Clerk: M. Goodman, Not Reported. (302/JMQ)
